title: Det finns ingen hemlig hemlig sås
text:
# There Is No Secret Sauce

As a child, I looked up to my parents. They knew everything and had answers to all my questions. Teachers at school were the same way—they possessed all the knowledge, which they then generously sprinkled over the ignorant masses of minions. "One day I'll be like them and know everything," I thought.

Guess what? That day never came. I grew up, but the feeling of having all the answers never arrived. On the contrary, it got even worse when I had my first child. I didn't know a damn thing about how to raise a child. I wasn't a superhuman. I had no answers about the meaning of life or what to do when you grow up. I just winged it from one day to the next. My child became both a test subject and the answer key. What worked brilliantly one day was completely ineffective the next.

But I got good at one thing: keeping the panic at bay. When I didn't have control of the situation, I just pretended I knew what I was doing. "We're not lost at all. We're just taking an unexplored detour."

You just have to accept it: all parents throughout all of history have just faked it. They had no control or plan. They just closed their eyes and drove forward, did exactly what their parents did, or the complete opposite, and hoped their way was the right one. There is no secret sauce, just like in Kung Fu Panda. No Morpheus handing you the red pill and revealing reality. You're stuck in the Matrix and you know it.

What does this have to do with design?, you might ask. Quite a lot, actually. I realized the same pattern existed at work too. As a junior designer, I looked up to my older, more experienced colleagues with fancy titles and high salaries. Like a little bird, I gaped for answers to my questions and for the magic formula to banish my constant insecurity.

But guess what? Your senior colleagues are faking it too. They don't have absolute answers or control of the situation. At best, they have educated guesses based on previous experience. Sometimes it works, sometimes it doesn't. Same thing with big established companies. You'd think a venerable bank has a better grip on reality than a startup. But probably not. They're just as confused, making wild gambles and messing up constantly, no matter how many layers of middle management they've managed to create.

This sounds like a bunch of whining and bad news. But actually, it's a good thing. You don't have to wait anymore to become good enough, to know enough, or for some stone tablet with all the answers to come sailing down from heaven. Nobody knows what they're doing. An expert is right just as often (or rarely) as a layperson. If someone succeeded, they either got lucky or failed a hundred times before finally getting it right.

The best way to gain more control is to accept that you can never have complete control and plan for every circumstance. The only way forward is to just go for it and handle things as they come. "Roll with the punches," as they say in English.

title: Amazons bästa produkt är ett skämt
text:
# Amazon's Best Product is a Joke

Amazon's Best Product is a Joke
Created on 2021-10-27 16:35
Published on 2021-10-27 17:27

A warning. I'm about to dissect a really good SNL sketch, and in doing so it will probably stop being funny. If you haven't already seen it, do that before you continue reading.

Amazon Silver on SNL.

I laughed hard the first time I saw the sketch. Pensioners are just so (unintentionally) funny. The new technology, so obvious to us, is like magic to an old person. They don't understand anything anyway.

Then it hit me. The reason it's funny is that the combination of old people and new technology creates such a contrast. But why do they have to be opposites? Advanced, voice-controlled AI devices weren't developed for shits and giggles. Rarely has a unicorn startup boasted about seniors as their primary target demographic. Strange, since more and more people are becoming older and older, so there should be money to be made. The paradox is that many of the elderly's needs are actually satisfied by Alexa Silver. So instead of laughing at the old folks, we should kick-start production. We have ed-tech and med-tech now—what we need is old-tech.

So what would Amazon Silver offer?

**Generous error margins.**

You can say Alexa, Odessa, Alexandra or Collessa. The device understands. Maybe you could set your own name. It understands what you mean despite instructions coming in the wrong order or being incoherent.

**Clarity.**

It speaks loud and clearly, doesn't mind repeating itself and rephrasing. It understands that different contexts have different meanings.

**Companionship.**

Loneliness is a major problem that affects the elderly especially hard. Family rarely visits, friends are few, and any care staff are always in a hurry to get to the next resident. Here, a constantly present conversation partner—or at least a listening partner—would be perfect.

**Health and wellbeing.**

How is it possible that people die alone and it takes days before anyone discovers it? A smart device could monitor how the person is doing, remind them about medication, track their movement in the apartment, regulate the apartment's temperature, measure heart rate (in combination with a smart watch), call for help and much more. The device could encourage activity if someone has been sitting too long or social contact if they haven't spoken to anyone for a while.

**Personal assistant.**

Finally someone to help remind them about activities like doctor's appointments, medication, and when relatives are visiting. Making it easy to locate important things like phone, wallet, and keys. Helping with shopping, booking haircuts, transportation services, family visits, etc.

All this functionality is certainly possible to achieve today if you're technically gifted and have the time and money it requires. But I haven't seen it all collected and packaged in a smooth, user-friendly product at an affordable price, where everything just works out of the box. Until then, the idea remains just a sketch on SNL and we get to keep laughing at these silly old folks. Like here: OK Goo Goo. ;-)

title: Still faking it
text:
# Still faking it

I've always had a hard time with the expression "Fake it til you make it". It felt so inauthentic. So fake ;-) Why should you pretend to be something you're not? Surely it's better to actually get good at something first and then be able to say with pride that you can do it, instead of just pretending.

In many cases, that's true. I don't want to be operated on by a doctor who's still learning from their mistakes, or sit on a plane with a pilot who's experimenting. But for many other, less critical cases, it's the fastest way forward and sometimes the only way. Take for example the eternal chicken-or-egg dilemma: you can't get a job without experience and you can't get experience without a job. If you're not lucky enough to have a very lenient employer, you need to trick your way into that first job. "Yeah, I've done loads of that, no problem." Then rush home, ninja-Google what it was they wanted you to know, learn it in record time, and be anxious for the first month. But then you're in the matrix.

Faking is a psychological mechanism you can exploit as a life hack. Imagine you want to start jogging regularly. Right now you're an ordinary, unfit person who wants to get in shape. You also think and act like an ordinary unfit person who wants to get in shape. All your decisions are made from that perspective.

But what if you pretend to be what you want to become? By faking your new identity as a jogger (which you absolutely are not yet), you can influence your thought patterns and your behavior. When asked "Should I go out and jog?", your old ordinary self would answer "nah, it's raining, better do it tomorrow. I'm also a bit tired from work." But you're now your new "self", the jogger, who answers "how nice a bit of rain would be" and cheerfully puts on running shoes and a rain jacket.

Want to quit smoking? Answer "No thanks, I don't smoke" next time you're offered a cigarette. And it's true—your new "self" doesn't smoke. Now it's just a matter of your old "self" catching up.

The biggest fake of all must be parenthood. Every day you're supposed to play a person who has it together, who remembers packed lunches, parent-teacher meetings, and buying new rubber boots. Someone who sets sensible, fair, and consistent rules, who's always there and solves every problem and crisis. Maybe there are some superhuman people who actually have it together. The rest of us just take life as it comes and fake it. Every child is really a huge social experiment, and for the most part, it works out pretty well.

So keep on faking, you will make it.

title: På tal om innovation
text:
# Speaking of Innovation

Speaking of Innovation
Created on 2020-12-15 16:39
Published on 2020-12-15 17:10

All companies want to innovate. Or rather, they have to innovate. The pace of development is constantly accelerating – new hardware, new software, constant updates, new customer groups with new needs, demands and expectations. But how do you innovate in the right way? Most established companies have their niche, their USPs and their sacred cows – or rather golden cows that they want to continue milking to recoup their previous investments. How much should you invest in new ideas that might turn out to be dead ends?

Innovation is difficult. Not just the act of innovating, but also innovating in the right amount. Because innovative products that are ahead of their time feel foreign or even frightening to consumers. Segway and Google Glass were very innovative and ahead of both their time and their competitors. But that was also precisely what meant that neither need, recognition, nor the social codes were in place. Imagine arriving on your dizzying Segway with a brand new pair of Google Glasses on your nose. Would you be seen as a cool innovator or a nerdy wannabe? Can you wear your glasses while having coffee with a friend without seeming pretentious? All this uncertainty means that the general public doesn't dare buy a completely unknown product. It remains an oddity among the *innovators* and *early adopters*.

So the trick is to stay at the forefront without falling over the edge. How do you do it? I have no idea. (Just because I'm writing this article doesn't mean I have all the answers.) So I asked some people who do. I welcome **Peter Sandberg**, who works with innovation at Rise and hosts the podcast Spinnovation, **Johan Berndtsson**, founder of inUse and now a freelance consultant in innovation and management, and **Maria Idebro**, management consultant at THREAD.

**What's the most common question you get about innovation?**

**Peter:** I rarely get the same question over and over, but many of my guests on the Spinnovation podcast have emphasized the importance of innovation being something that must come from the top. Without management, and also the board, being involved in enabling innovation and standing behind innovation work at all levels, it becomes very difficult to bring about change.

**Maria:** A common question is whether it's smartest/most efficient to have innovation separate or integrated. In other words, whether you should have, for example, an innovation lab working on the future alongside other operations, or whether it's something everyone should be involved with in some way.

**Johan:** It depends on who's asking. If it's people lower down in the organization, it's often about how to get attention around the challenges they see. They're looking for a way to get leaders to feel a sense of urgency. If the question comes from management level, it's usually about how you can work together with customers to identify new opportunities for the business.

**Has the view of innovation changed recently, particularly during 2020?**

**Peter:** I don't think the view of innovation has changed that much. However, many organizations have been forced to become more innovative thanks to Corona. Perhaps not so much in what they offer, but in the way they work. All crises contribute to increased innovation. Another thing is that there's actually now a standard for innovation management, and people can now be personally certified as innovation leaders. This is a sign that innovation is being taken more seriously and that innovation management is shifting from being a role to becoming a profession.

**Maria:** 2020 has certainly "forced" many to innovate in different ways. What I think might be lasting is that we've gotten a very clear example of how the world can change quickly. Overnight, the behavior of customers, suppliers, or collaboration partners can change. It becomes clear that you need to be able to build capacity to pivot quickly.

**Johan:** Since I left inUse at the end of 2019, I've worked a lot with online grocery retail. A very interesting industry, not least because high picking and distribution costs eat up the originally low margins. When the coronavirus caused demand to skyrocket, it suddenly became crucial to find offerings that add more value to the customer – offerings they're therefore willing to pay more for. What's required for this is processes that make it possible to work research-driven and results-driven.

**There are so many ongoing trends and rapid development. How do you know which direction to innovate in?**

**Peter:** You can never know for sure. It can be useful to compare chess and poker. Chess is complicated but poker is complex. The difference lies in access to information. In chess, both players, and even any spectators, have access to all information. There's no hidden information. In poker there's much information that's unknown – what cards the other players have, which have been discarded, and which remain in the deck. This lack of information is what makes poker complex. The same analogy applies to companies and innovation. Building a jumbo jet isn't complex for a company like Boeing – it's "just" complicated because they have all the information and knowledge needed. Building a new product or service is complex because there's so much unknown information you don't yet have about, for example, the market, customer needs, technologies, etc. You can never be completely certain you're doing the right thing. The solution in that case is to have a good innovation process to rely on that enables rapid experiments and quick hypothesis testing.

**Maria:** Combine gathering data with actively deciding which direction you want to innovate in. 1. Here's what we know. 2. What do we want? What company do we want to be? And then work agile so you continuously keep learning and experimenting.

**Johan:** My sense is that companies, employees and management, have fairly good ideas rooted in what's happening in their own industry. If you complement these with qualitative and quantitative studies of customer behavior, and with analyses of broader trends, you have a fairly good picture to start from. Once you've formed a hypothesis about where you want to go, it's "just" a matter of testing your way forward in small steps. But... This is where it usually falls apart. In the implementation. Sometimes because there are unclear roles and responsibilities, but usually because you lack working methods, processes and routines when it comes to testing in small steps. Pilot studies that could be carried out over days or weeks become projects that take months, or years, to implement.

**Do you have to innovate yourself? Can't you just ride on other people's innovations that prove to work?**

**Peter:** Yes, you can build very successful companies based on other people's innovations or even just by copying successful concepts. We see examples of this everywhere, such as consulting firms, staffing agencies and gaming sites. However, that's not innovation because nothing new that didn't exist before has been created.

**Maria:** You probably need to have some innovative strength anyway so you're not caught flatfooted if circumstances change. But perhaps not everyone needs to be super innovative. A good trick can be to ride the waves created by someone in the forefront. Oh, you're going to Mars? Maybe we can be the ones who livestream the landing. Or develop a solution so you can eat popcorn in zero gravity.

**Johan:** It depends on how large the market is and how late you arrive at the table. Often it works fine to just ride on others, but if it's an industry and market where many players are crowded together, it becomes very difficult to just copy. Then you have to find some other value, like a truly unique brand, but in context that's not much of a moat, and the risk is that you fall behind when customers decide someone else is trendier.

**How can you tell the difference between good and bad innovation / ways of innovating?**

**Peter:** You could almost say by definition that bad innovation is something that isn't used or gets no demand. A bad way to innovate is to develop something that's technology-driven but doesn't solve a concrete problem or need.

**Maria:** Maybe I'm not answering the question exactly, but I think values will play an increasingly important role going forward. A bad innovation process is one where you lose sight of your values and only steer by other things.

**Johan:** Bad innovation isn't used. Money down the drain for whoever invested, and for society and the environment a gadget or service that took resources and takes up space unnecessarily. Bad ways to innovate... There are many, but perhaps the ethical perspective is the most important to highlight here. I'm a big fan of agile working methods and testing your way forward, but you have to be careful that it doesn't happen at people's expense. "Move fast and break things" or "fail fast" isn't okay if it's about people's feelings, relationships, money, or health. On a small scale you can cause extreme stress. A Facebook post encouraging someone to celebrate their dead daughter's sixth birthday… A bank account that suddenly has zero balance on the same day as bills need to be paid… On a larger scale you can cause suicide, or even genocide, as Zuckerberg and Facebook have shown. Innovation requires care.

**Should you invest in an R&D department or should the entire company innovate?**

**Peter:** Many companies have scaled back their own R&D to instead invest in open innovation, which means going outside the organization to find external solutions and solution providers. In some companies, innovation is driven more by the marketing department, which through their close contact with customers is often the first to pick up on new needs. All employees can and should be involved in innovation work, but it also puts demands on management, culture and organization for it to work.

**Maria:** Ha! I was kind of answering that one as a response to an earlier question! :) I think you should innovate decentralized as much as possible. But maybe a combination.

**Johan:** Yes. To be successful you need to have some centralized in-house capacity, but you also need to involve the whole company. There's simply so incredibly much valuable knowledge out there, and it would be wasteful not to take advantage of it. Finding simple ways to engage the whole organization is also a good way to build culture, energy and cohesion. Beyond that you need to work with both partners and customers. Partners because you rarely have all the competence needed to create a complete solution, especially today when innovation is so much about building ecosystems of services where you rarely have full control over everything. Customers have to be involved because ultimately it's they who will buy and use the services that are developed.

**How much of your time or budget should you invest in innovation?**

**Peter:** There's no exact answer, but I think 5% is a reasonable minimum.

**Johan:** I don't have a good answer here... So I'll ask a counter-question instead. If a software developer where 50% of the workforce continuously works on developing and refining the service they offer – are they working with innovation? Or is it only the 2% working on "future projects" who are innovating? ;)

**Is there anything else I've forgotten to ask, or something you'd like to add?**

**Peter:** Jack Welch, the legendary CEO of GE, said decades ago that "When the pace of change on the outside is greater than on the inside, you can start anticipating the end." I don't think anyone thinks the pace of change is lower today compared to then. That's why innovation has never been more important.

Thanks so much for your wise answers.

title: Många goda råd som jag själv inte följt.
text:
# Many Good Pieces of Advice That I Myself Haven't Followed

Created on 2020-12-01 10:23
Published on 2020-12-01 10:48

I only managed three weeks of regular blogging. There were fist pumps, high fives, and cheers. And what happens now? I stumble and fail to deliver. Sure, it's not a big deal—the sun still rises and nobody has really noticed that I haven't posted anything. It's hardly as serious as skipping rent or being late with the kids' pocket money. That's when you can talk about consequences.

But for me it felt like a defeat. If only I had planned better, started earlier, and so on. But I can squeeze some lemonade out of this—at least I got a good idea for my overdue post.

Creating new positive habits is difficult.

An important rule (which I absolutely have not followed) is to start simple. Something so simple that you can't fail at it. Instead of doing ten push-ups, which can be tough if you're out of shape, start with one. Pretty much everyone can do one a day. Then you build slowly and gradually from there.

For my part, as of now I'm setting aside a quarter of an hour each day to write. I can manage that. I don't need to finish the entire article in a quarter hour, but after a couple of quarters it should be ready. To make it even easier, I'm setting an alarm for exactly the same time every (work)day.

Next tip (which I also haven't followed).

Expect that you're going to stumble. It will happen sooner or later, so don't be caught off guard. For some reason we (I, at least) always plan from an optimal scenario. When I estimate how long a job will take, I only think about the straight path. How long does it take from A to B. I don't account for the fact that I don't know the route, where the destination is, uncertainty factors, miscalculations, the mental work, all the mistakes needed to find the right way. The reason is that we base ourselves on the final results from before, which seem so very simple and obvious. All the iterations, discussions, and detours that were necessary to get there—you've already forgotten about them.

For my part, I have to account for the fact that it takes longer than I think. I need to create strategies for how I'll act if things don't go as planned. If I miss posting an entry one week, it's not a failure—it's just a bump in the road. The important thing is to come back and make sure I write at least one post the week after.

The last tip (yes, I know).

Set clear sub-goals. "Starting to write" is a very abstract goal. You never really finish—it just goes on and on. If you want to learn to play an instrument, a good sub-goal could be to learn a simple song by heart. It becomes immediately concrete, you know when you're done, and then you just set the next sub-goal.

My goal is to write 20 posts. I can manage that. Then we'll see. ;-)

Bonus tip 1.

Reward yourself with a reward after each completed sub-goal. A piece of chocolate, a cup of latte, or 15 minutes of video games. Suddenly it feels much more fun, even if you've stumbled at the finish line.

Bonus tip 2.

Listen to the podcast Monkey Mindset for more tips on mental training and personal development. https://www.monkeymindset.se/

title: Lära sig genom att lära ut, eller hur jag slutade vara en glad idiot.
text:
# Learning by Teaching, or How I Stopped Being a Blissfully Ignorant Fool

That sounds very paradoxical, I thought when I heard the claim for the first time. How can you teach something when you don't know it? I trust someone with solid experience more than a green beginner.

But there's something to it. It's one thing to know something yourself and something completely different to be able to explain it to someone else. Take directions, for example. You know exactly how to get to the nearest subway station or grocery store. But when you try to explain it to a lost person, the obvious suddenly becomes less obvious. How many intersections would you pass and what was the name of that crossing street anyway?

An explanation requires fundamental understanding as well as a pedagogical structure and layout. If you want someone else to understand something, you need to start from the ground up, step by step at a reasonable pace, preferably with examples and anchored in reality or something your "student" already knows. This isn't something I invented. It's a popular pedagogical method, classified under collaborative learning, where you learn together, from and with each other.

But I obviously had no idea about any of this when I said yes to teaching the course User-Centered Design at Nackademin. I knew that stuff about UCD and UX, after all. I've worked with it for years. And Design Thinking, well I was familiar with that. I've seen the model countless times in different forms and variations.

How hard could it be?

In hindsight, I'm glad I didn't know how little I knew. I was at the bottom of the knowledge pyramid, a blissfully ignorant fool unaware of my own incompetence. "How hard could it be? It's just a matter of turning my knowledge into a few slides."

Famous last words. It took me a month of intensive studying and reviewing before I started putting all the puzzle pieces together and slowly climbing the pyramid. Suddenly there was context—what had previously been loose, rattling puzzle pieces formed into a clear picture. There was cause and effect, differences and similarities, before and after. Now I was ready. And yes, it was difficult, but definitely worth it.

So what has this learning taught me?

You don't need to be an expert to teach. It could be just holding a workshop, giving a presentation, or running a lunch and learn.

It takes an insanely long time to create material and do planning. So start early and let it take time. This is where you discover any gaps in your knowledge.

Prioritize and create focus. What's important, what comes first, what should people take with them? How does it all connect?

Pedagogy and design have a lot in common. As a teacher, you have the opportunity to design others' future knowledge. How cool is that?

Increased confidence, both in the subject matter but also in being the one who leads, takes responsibility, and sets the agenda. Most importantly, overcoming the Swedes' greatest fear—"speaking in front of people." (Actually it's number two, since number one is death).

Learning in a group. All the ideas, questions, suggestions, and discussions with the students are very inspiring.

Disclaimer: I'm not a trained teacher, I lack pedagogical education and a "real" teaching job. I work full-time as a product designer and the course at Nackademin is a side project. If I were to call myself something pedagogical, it would be course leader. What I do have, however, is a strong interest in pedagogy and in sharing my knowledge and experiences.

title: Män är från Mars, kvinnorna från Venus och kunderna från helvetet.
text:
# Men Are from Mars, Women Are from Venus, and Customers Are from Hell.

Created on 2020-12-07 16:36
Published on 2020-12-07 17:02

It has almost become a sport for designers to express anger and frustration over their work not receiving the appreciation it deserves. Customers who are never satisfied, come with endless revisions and slowly but surely transform a perfect design into a train wreck. "Can you make the logo bigger? Just a liiiittle bit, and a liiiittle bit more."

If it's not the client causing trouble, it's the boss, the project manager, or someone else who wants to "add" some fancy design to a random project.

This frustration has taken shape in countless memes, forum complaints, songs, YouTube videos, and even this entertaining collection at https://clientsfromhell.net/

Most stories create great recognition and make you laugh and cry, often simultaneously. I've myself spent late nights making revisions I happened to promise and now paid dearly for.

What all these scenarios have in common is that they're always told from the designer's perspective. The customer is the antagonist who, out of spite, ignorance, or plain stupidity, causes problems for our design hero. The customer doesn't even get to give their version of events, only bears the responsibility for the entire situation. This way you build trenches, walls and strengthen the "us-versus-them" mentality.

It can definitely be entertaining reading with many bad situations and misunderstandings. And now comes the part. Wait for it.....

BUT....

This is not a solution. Not even a good problem statement. It's just a symptom of poor communication. It's surely obvious that your client can't know as much about design as you do, that they can't tell the difference between Futura and Helvetica or that they want their logo to be visible. If they knew as much as you did, they would have done it themselves and saved themselves the trouble and the money.

You need to work with what the client has and what they can do. They know their business and they have a vision where your design serves a purpose. To succeed with your assignment, you need to understand both the client's current situation and what they want to achieve with your help. You need to create a common language, a babelfish that translates the client's business goals into design.

Does the client say the logo should be bigger? Perfect. Let them explain what they expect a bigger logo to accomplish. Maybe it should make it very clear which page the visitor has landed on, create brand awareness, showcase their new logo, etc. Is there another solution, like clever copy that both mentions the brand and explains the customer benefit? Or a compromise where the logo is large on the landing page but not elsewhere on the site?

As soon as the designer starts actively listening and involves the client in the design process, a synergy is created. It's no longer an order and delivery situation, it becomes collaboration and co-creation. The client will feel seen, understood, and most importantly, get what they want and need.

You don't have to agree with everything your client says. Clients are wrong, gladly and often. The key is communicating that in a smart way without being condescending. Or the worst of all—doing exactly what they ask and then getting blamed for the poor result. Instead, suggest alternative solutions with the same goal. Or why not test it—for example, an A/B test pits your client's idea and your design against each other. If the client wins, they get to feel smart; if you win, you have objective arguments for your case.

Next time you think your client is really bad at understanding design, you can ask yourself how good you are at communicating. It's not the client's job to understand design, but it is your job to explain it.

title: Världens kortaste inlägg
text:
# The World's Shortest Post

Created on 2021-04-21 07:34
Published on 2021-04-21 07:50

How's the blogging going anyway? my guilty conscience asks a bit irritably.

So-so, I answer. I knew exactly what I wanted to write. A good, well-formulated, researched post about feedback.

So when it didn't happen, nothing happened at all? No plan B?

Exactly, I answer. All or nothing.

I keep falling into the same pitfalls I warn others about. There's no point in me reading all these books about habits and behavior if I don't translate them into action. Knowledge that doesn't affect behavior is just information.

That's precisely why this post will be extra short. So that it actually gets written. Proof in itself that it's better to do something small often than something big very rarely. The consequences of missing a short daily session are far less than missing the week's bootcamp.

Doing something simple and often is a perfect cure for perfectionism, performance pressure, and writer's block. Focus on frequency and quantity instead of grand gestures and quality, and all demands and expectations disappear. The magical part is that quality comes free. You become good on the sly without having to strain yourself to the breaking point.

Try it yourself. Spend 15 minutes a day on something new, like learning Spanish on Duolingo the way I am. You'll be amazed how much you've learned after two months. Let me know how it goes. Hasta la vista.

title: Vad kunden egentligen vill ha och hur du ska ta reda på det.
text:
# What the Customer Really Wants and How to Find Out

Have you also heard that it's important to deliver beyond the customer's expectations? Usually it's interpreted to mean you should work extra hard, give 110%. Nothing wrong with that. But if everyone else is also giving 110%, then that's what customers expect. So your 110% is just 100% in the customer's eyes. Then you need to give 120% for it to be noticed. Meanwhile, you only get paid for 100%. In other words, you're giving away a fifth of your work for free. It seems like a bad deal and a shortcut to burnout.

What's even stranger is that everyone who wants to deliver beyond the customer's expectations often doesn't even know what the customer expects. It's like doing high jump with an invisible bar. How the hell are you supposed to know how high to aim? There's nothing more frustrating than when you've put in a lot of work, followed the customer's wishes to the letter, and get the response: "Nope, that's not what I wanted."

The tricky thing about expectations is that they're usually unspoken. Especially if they form the foundation of an assignment or project. You're easily fooled into thinking that detailed specifications, success metrics, lists of features and budgets also fulfill the customer's expectations. But they're just hygiene factors. Expectations lie beyond these documents, sometimes embedded in the customer's subconscious. That's why every major project should start with this simple question.

What does the customer expect to happen when the project is done?

Forget about obstacles for a moment, all the features that need to be built, all the schedules that need to be kept or problems that need to be solved. Stop selling, pitching, planning and presenting for a moment. Transport yourself and the customer into the future. The project is done, everything has gone according to plan and the expected effects have taken shape. What is different now than before the project?

Your only job is to understand why this vision matters to the customer. What does it mean for users, for business and for the brand. What new opportunities open up? What would happen if the project didn't happen or failed? Write down all the effects and outcomes the customer is hoping for. Regardless of how big or small they seem.

The next question is equally important, and here we bring the customer back to reality. Now it's time to start kicking the tires and turn those fluffy dreams into something concrete.

What needs to happen to make it real?

The idea isn't that you should end up with a detailed timeline. The main points are enough. Together you can distinguish between what's a requirement and what's a vague hope. Are the customer's expectations reasonable or do they need adjusting? Are there points outside your control? Which ones are really difficult or time-consuming? Which are the most risky? What do we need to tackle first? Can we break down the journey into different stages?

The final task is to boil down the expectation to one or a couple of sentences. What is the absolutely most important thing that should happen for the customer to feel satisfied. This becomes the customer's MVE, Minimal Viable Expectations. Now it's just a matter of regularly verifying throughout the project that your delivery really meets the customer's MVE. If too big a gap appears, the work or the MVE needs to be adjusted asap. The important thing is that the delivery and expectations are in sync when the project reaches the finish line.

Still want to deliver beyond the customer's expectations? Just include one of the less important points and add it as a bonus. Mission accomplished.

title: 7 habits of highly mediocre people
text:
# 7 Habits of Highly Mediocre People

Created on 2022-05-28 10:30
Published on 2022-05-28 11:00

## 1. Define the scale yourself

If you're classified as mediocre, it's because you're being measured against a scale that someone else has determined. You can only accept the situation and follow the rules of the game. If it's important to you to be the best at something, create your own scale. You have every opportunity to become your company's master at Fortnite or beer tasting.

## 2. What matters most isn't measurable

The truly important values in life can't be measured. You can't be the best at love or win the World Championship in happiness. In other words, we're all superstars or mediocrities. These values are also highly individual. What makes one person happy doesn't have to work for anyone else. Only you know what works for you. You can always decide to become the best parent, friend, or boss that you can be.

## 3. Efficiency isn't efficient

We're constantly urged to become more efficient, agile, productive, and innovative. With new technology, we create new opportunities to pump out even more products. We search for the promised land of happiness that eternal growth has promised us. But the sad truth is that we don't become happier, just more stressed, and we squander our resources and the planet's resources completely unnecessarily. What if the answer is the opposite? Instead of working more, work less and spend your time on something that actually makes you happy. (With respect to all single parents struggling to make rent.)

## The world needs mediocrities

Zlatan needs his fans more than the fans need Zlatan. We are the ones who create and carry the superstars. What would all the TikTok stars be without their fans and likes? Who would consume all the content? You and I play an important role in the equation. It's thanks to us regular folks that superstars exist. Next time, it'll be you signing Zlatan's jersey.

## It's uneconomical to be the best

It costs extremely much to be the best. Most people aren't prepared to make the sacrifices required to reach the top. Many try, but only few succeed. If you against all odds get there, you have nothing more to win or prove, but everything to lose. It takes just as much energy to stay at the top and fight against everyone else who wants your place.

Instead of mastering just one thing, you can use the same time and energy to become completely decent or even quite good at many different areas. You also avoid betting on the wrong horse. Being an expert on combustion engines today might not be the most fun.

## You are your own superstar

A superstar as inspiration can be motivating, but the realization that you'll never reach their level is demoralizing. If you always compare yourself to the best, you'll always be inadequate. Why even try? No wonder most children quit their sports or hobbies when all the fun disappears and only demands and high expectations remain.

If instead you start from yourself, you are right now always your best self. You don't need to do anything to be the best, win prizes or titles. You do it because you want to.

## Embrace your inner mediocrity

Words like mediocre, average, half-hearted, and acceptable have a negative ring to them. They mean that something or someone hasn't lived up to their full potential. At the same time, there are words like adequate, balanced, measured, and sufficient. They mean you've given it effort but haven't overdone it. The same thing but with two completely different values. Now it's up to you what you choose to fill your inner mediocrity with. Fill it up, but not completely full. There should always be room for a little more.

## A few tips:

Want to be inspired? Read *The 7 Habits of Highly Effective People*.

Want advice that works and gets you to appreciate what you have? Read *The Subtle Art of Not Giving a F*ck*.

title: Systemet som Gud (Daniel Kahneman) glömde
text:
# The System That God (Daniel Kahneman) Forgot

Created on 2022-03-30 14:37
Published on 2022-03-30 14:55

I really appreciate Susan Weinschenk's new course in behavioral design on Ambition Empower. The first lesson was an introduction to how we make our decisions. If you're familiar with Daniel Kahneman's book, *Thinking, Fast and Slow*, then you know the concept of the two systems of thought. System 1, the fast, energy-efficient way that builds on habits, impulses and unconscious decisions. System 2, the slow and analytical approach that requires time and energy and is reserved for difficult or important decisions. We use one or the other. Either we go on autopilot or we stop and think things through. Both ways are really good and complement each other excellently. The only downside with the concept is the confusing naming convention. Wouldn't fast and slow have worked, like in the book title? Now I have to think slowly every time I'm trying to remember which system was which.

What do I mean by System 3? If System 1 is our habits and System 2 is our logical thinking, then System 3 is the human factor where our personality, feelings and ego have the final say. System 3 is where System 1 and System 2 meet, both mathematically and conceptually. System 3 kicks in where logic no longer suffices, like choosing which partner to be with or deciding which kitten is the cutest. These are thoughtful choices based on gut feeling rather than logic.

According to Susan, we use either one system or the other when making a choice. A product should therefore be designed based on which system will be used most. For example, a bag of chips should tempt spontaneous purchases and is hardly an important decision. Typical System 1. The design needs to be expressive, easy to understand and speak directly to the customer. If, on the other hand, we're designing for choosing a pension fund, the priorities will be completely different. Here we need to create security and trust and provide a lot of information before the user can make a choice.

For System 3, we take all the feelings from System 1 and let them override all the logic in System 2. A perfect example is the first iPhone. When it was presented, it was inferior to its competitors on nearly every point. It lacked a physical keyboard, had poor coverage, terrible battery life and only a couple of pre-installed apps. Yet it got customers to look past all these shortcomings and long to swipe their fingers across the (at that time) giant screen.

All luxury goods require a bigger (financial) decision, but the seller simultaneously doesn't want you to start thinking too much. That's why they sneak past your common sense (for example by not even showing or mentioning the price) and go straight for your ego. Why should you buy this extremely expensive watch or this ridiculously fuel-guzzling sports car? Because you're worth it. Because you can finally be yourself. Because everyone else will see you. Because no Nobel Prize winner is going to come and tell you how you should and shouldn't think. Fast or slow? I think the way I want to. So they say.

title: Being a designer is not a joke
text:
Being a designer is not a joke
Being a designer is not a joke
Created on 2019-11-28 10:49
Published on 2019-11-28 11:11
How many designers does it take to change a light bulb?
- Does it have to be a light bulb?
Have you heard it before? Many of my (non designer) colleagues laughed with recognition. They see designers as children with crayons, drawing pretty pictures, asking silly questions and don’t really producing anything real. When confronted with a single, mundane task, designers get stuck. And the joke visualize that in a very striking and funny way.
I have a totally different interpretation of that joke, and unfortunately it’s not funny at all. Sorry. To me it's the definition of being a designer and design thinker. We designers avoid doings things on autopilot, without reflecting over the purpose. We start by asking questions: "Can it be done in a different, more efficient, accessible, sustainable or fun way? What is the purpose of changing the bulb? Who is going to use it, when, how and why? What kind of light should the bulb produce? Can it do anything more, like change color, intensity or blink? Should it behave differently in the morning, evening, during the night or when nobody is at home? Are there any other possible sources of light?
This base of understanding is source of our inspiration. Then we start prototyping, testing and observing. Without understanding the problem we can not evaluate the result. This is the power of design. To push thing forward and open new possibilities where others see a single solution.
Finding answers to all those question is fascinating, inspiring and drives innovation. But it takes time. Because time is a limited resource, people and companies don’t want to waste it. Your clients wants to see result and final art screenshots, not personas and post-its on a whiteboard. Your jobb is to educate, communicate and involve your boss, clients and colleagues in your design process. Show that those seemingly non productive actions are a vital part of the final solution. Make them co-creators and a part of the process.
The goal is to make them to ask the question: Does it have to be a light bulb?

title: Should hiring be more like dating?
text:
Should hiring be more like dating?
Should hiring be more like dating?
Created on 2026-05-06 11:41
Published on 2026-05-06 11:57
One question kept popping up while I was doing the interviews for the Ghosted Report. This can't be a unique problem. There need to be others struggling with exactly the same thing.
"What other area spends this much energy trying to connect two humans to see if they are a perfect match?"
So I called Olga Engwall to find out. She is a former UX colleague, now building MinglMe, a with fresh take on dating. I needed to know: have they actually solved the problem of translating data into feelings? Is there a way to use AI for more than just efficiency and automation? Or are we all just stuck in the same trap?
I needed a new perspective. I got more than one.
Olga has spent years thinking about why people connect, and more specifically, why the systems we built to help them connect keep getting in the way. Her diagnosis of dating apps is sharp: they turned human connection into a volume problem. Swipe left, swipe right, five hundred people processed before breakfast. The apps make it feel like efficiency. What it actually produces is numbness.
Every major dating app is owned by the same company, Match Group, and they all have the same basic design underneath the different branding. Some sell themselves as casual, some as serious. All of them keep you in the app as long as possible, because that is how they make money. A match is actually bad for the business model. Like a medicine that does not want to get you healthy, just keep you taking more of it.
Sounds familiar?
The Ghosted report found the same mechanics running through hiring. Candidates send applications into the void. Recruiters drown in noise they cannot convert into anything useful. Both sides perform for a system that serves neither of them. Olga summary was insightful.
"You hire A person without THE person. The personality gets removed from the process."
It is a good line. I heard similar versions of it from designers and recruiters who had never met her.
Dating apps let you filter on height, age, interests, distance. Hiring does the same with years of experience, job titles, keyword matching. Both create the feeling of control while completely bypassing the thing that actually decides whether it works: what happens when two people are in a room together.
Olga cited research on this. Even a perfect match on Big Five personality scores, the most scientifically validated personality model we have, tells you almost nothing about chemistry. Two people can score identically across all five dimensions and still sit across from each other and feel absolutely nothing.
You cannot quantify the moment it clicks.
MingleMe is built on exactly this idea. Instead of matching people on profiles and hoping for the best, the app puts people in shared activities. Cooking class, escape room, pub quiz, whatever. You buy a ticket. The chat opens. You show up. The activity is the thing, not the date. You are busy for an hour doing something together, and in that hour you see things no profile could ever show: how someone handles losing a game, how they treat a stranger, whether they are generous or stingy with praise. You cannot keep the front window up for ninety minutes while also trying to crack a code with four people you just met.
Olga's phrase for what this creates: "You see who they actually are."
A designer I interviewed for the Ghosted report, without knowing anything about MinglMe, said almost the same thing about his first in-person visit at a company he later joined. He saw the office. He watched how people greeted each other in the corridor. He got a coffee and felt whether this place could be part of his life. The formal interview mattered less than those first twenty minutes of just being there. A one designer put it:
"You taste the coffee. That tells you more than anything they ask you."
Two people from completely different worlds landing on the same metaphor. That is not a coincidence. That is a signal.
There are no soulmates
Both in dating and hiring there is this illusion of the perfect candidate. One person who solves everything. Your job is just to find them.
In reality, people with totally different skills, personality, and ways of thinking can all solve the same problem, but have completely different effects on the people around them and the organization they join. Not better or worse. Just different. One might strengthen what is already there. One might challenge it. One might bring something the job description never thought to ask for.
Most hiring processes are designed to fill a role as written. Olga is arguing for something harder: look past the current problem to where you are actually heading. The difference is between filling a position and making a bet on a direction, where the direction matters more than the starting point.
That thinking is not in any ATS system I have ever seen.
Hard skills are the entry ticket, not the destination. Your diploma, your experience, your portfolio: they prove you can do the job. But they are backwards-looking. Like stocks, you cannot judge future performance only by looking at history. At some point you need to get into the room, see if you connect, find out whether you are actually heading in the same direction.
We do not marry someone who looks good on paper. We do not become close colleagues with someone we do not like or cannot collaborate with. When there is a genuine will to connect, the other problems become solvable.
I started this conversation looking for confirmation. I already believed the hiring system was broken and I wanted another perspective on why.
What I found was someone who had stopped asking that question. Olga is not trying to improve the filtering machinery. She is not building a better swipe. She looked at the problem and asked something different: how do we get people into a real situation together, as fast as possible?
That question does not exist in hiring yet.
It probably should.
The Ghosted report is at
designloop.se
. Olga Engwall is co-founder of
MinglMe
.

title: Merry Christmas and a Happy New Normal
text:
# Merry Christmas and a Happy New Normal

Created on 2020-12-23 10:20
Published on 2020-12-23 10:50

There is no stronger tradition than Christmas. It's the most Swedish thing we have (apart from Walpurgis Night and Midsummer). Many want to experience the magical feeling of Christmas that they enjoyed as children and pass that gift on to their own kids. Everything else can change as it likes, but no one touches Christmas. It is through these rituals and traditions that we create our identity and find a stable point in this chaotic existence. No wonder that Christmas 2020 seems more festive than ever, despite the lack of snow and holiday crowds. When we can't or aren't allowed to meet in person, people send Christmas cards and packages like never before, and all food delivery services have been fully booked for ages. We're going to show that damn corona how Christmas is supposed to be celebrated.

At the same time, the year 2020 shows us that normal is just a construction. Everything we're expected to be, how we should act, what's considered tasteful or tasteless—these are not absolute values or truths. It's an agreement that we've voluntarily or involuntarily signed with those around us. In other words, just someone else's idea that you've been made to take as your own. What was considered normal yesterday is seen as completely crazy today. Like giving your child a beating (preferably in public), playing professional hockey without a helmet, offering a pregnant woman a cigarette, or hugging a friend at a packed restaurant.

Sometimes these changes happen infinitely slowly (like with Christmas traditions), other times painfully fast (hello corona). But if this year has shown us anything, it's that we can change, when we really want to or when we really have to. If we really want to, we can stop burning rainforests, stop letting greed and profit margins dictate, stop destroying the planet, and instead be kinder, more generous, and let others win without us having to lose. We can even continue celebrating Christmas even though it will never be the same as before.

We just need to let go of our resistance and stop gazing wistfully back at the time before the pandemic. That time is over. Our job now is to create the new normal for next year. A new reality that is a great deal better than the normal of 2020 and before.

Have a really Merry Christmas and a Happy New Normal in 2021.

title: Vad gör en produktdesigner hela dagarna?
text:
# What Does a Product Designer Do All Day?

I was very nervous when, after eight years as a UX/UI consultant, I made the switch to working in-house as a product designer. My biggest fear was that I would get bored. As a consultant, I got to work with many clients, with great variation in both size, industry, level of detail, and project length. There was always a rush to dive into a new assignment, almost like a superhero arriving to save the day.

Suddenly working with the same product, day in and day out, sounded like a nightmare. How much can you really optimize a checkout flow, anyway?

My second worry was that I didn't actually know what a product designer really did. Quite a bit of impostor syndrome on that front. On paper, the jobs looked similar—create prototypes, test, talk to developers. So why was it called something different?

The first concern was answered almost immediately. There were so many different features to develop and test that I would hardly have time to get bored. The only thing that stayed the same was the graphic profile. That would also need reviewing, but that was a separate project.

The second concern took a bit longer to figure out. It wasn't about processes, tools, or deliverables. It was almost entirely about mindset. A kind of mental hierarchy. Just as UI is part of UX, I saw how UX became part of product design. In addition to caring for users' needs and experience, I also had to ensure the product grew, developed in the right direction, and continued to generate revenue. It wasn't a quick consultant gig, a hit-and-run. I would actually get to enjoy the results of all that work, see how the things I'd designed and been part of were received by users. I'd get the chance to fix any mistakes and work on new iterations. At the same time, I also had to live with all my previous decisions, so it was best to make sure they were as good as possible or could be changed down the line.

Now I'm in my second job as a product designer and I'm happier than ever. I was very careful to choose a product I believe in, at a company I'm comfortable working for, and in an industry that creates value and isn't just out for cash. That part is important. Think of it as a relationship. It's you and the product—you need to make each other happy for many years to come, stick with each other through both downturns and victories.

So to answer the question in the title. What do I actually do? I create flows in Miro, lead workshops, interview customers, gather feedback and wishes from customer success, stay in close contact with developers, prototype new features, do visual design, and right now I'm laying the foundation for an entirely new design system and product redesign. I've probably forgotten something...

Are you a UI and/or UX designer who wants to take the next step and find new challenges? Do you want to learn more about the business side of design? Then product designer might be a good choice. Small difference on the outside but big on the inside. You'll grow as a designer and broaden your horizons.

Besides, I have a secret to tell you. Just for you who've read this far, my dear, loyal reader. If you seriously want to give product design a shot and become my wingman, there's a link at the bottom. We'll be like Han and Chewie, Doc and Marty, or Emil and Alfred. We have some exciting times ahead: creating a new design system, building a design organization, connecting design with analytics, and taking a growing product to new heights. We'll give each other honest feedback, support, praise, inspire, and push each other outside our comfort zones. You could be an old UX fox or a rising star. I'm already looking forward to your application. See you soon.
