title: Designkritik är inte en förolämpning
text:
# Design Criticism Is Not an Insult

Created on 2022-02-22 11:37
Published on 2022-02-22 12:17

We creatives have been taught the importance of marketing and selling ourselves. Be your own brand, as they say. The customer buys you, not what you sell. Follow your heart and your passion. Find your own style, your voice and identity. Be proud of your work and know your worth. Good and well-intentioned advice, but with the side effect that you as a person, your skills and what you create all blend together. It becomes completely impossible to discuss the design without simultaneously judging you as a person.

So what can we do to sever the emotional bond to our work? How can we strengthen our confidence so we're not crushed by all the "well-meaning" criticism? How can we view a design presentation as something positive and not an interrogation?

Here are some insights that have helped me get better at receiving criticism.

**It's not about you**

You've struggled hard with your sketches, poured blood, sweat and tears into them. Of course you want your work to be well received. But here comes the twist. Your design was created by you but not for you. It will belong to the client, the developers and the users. Don't start your presentation with "now I'm going to present *my* design" but rather "here is a design solution to the problem."

**It's a team effort**

Even if you're the only designer in the project, the team, or the entire company, it's not you alone who should solve the entire problem. The others, regardless of role or title, are there with you. Include them early and often so everyone feels involved. Suddenly everyone is on board and shares responsibility for a successful outcome. It's not you and your design against the world, but you with the team against the problem, where design is an important part of the solution.

**Design is important but not everything**

Design may be your entire world. You've studied it for years—everything from typography, color theory to usability. It's big, it's difficult, and there are many decisions to make. But design is "just" one part of the final result. There are other parts that come before and after that are just as big and difficult as yours. Make sure you understand the entire process to ensure your design is relevant. Don't get upset if people don't understand the brilliance of your font choices or elegant layout. Instead, focus on what benefit it creates and how it helps you get closer to your goal.

**Be prepared**

Sometimes feedback comes spontaneously without you being ready. Then it's important to simply thank them for the input without getting defensive. The most common scenario is planned meetings with the client, the team, or other stakeholders. Think through what questions might come up and how you can answer them as factually as possible. What's the best outcome from the meeting and what can you do to make it happen? What's the worst-case scenario, how likely is it, and what can you do to prevent it? Prepare yourself mentally to stay calm and relaxed throughout the entire meeting.

**Be empathetic**

When empathy and design are mentioned in the same breath, it's almost exclusively about the users. Rarely does anyone mention empathy for your client, your team members, or yourself. But your design has consequences before it even reaches the users. Think about the developer who gets your sketches dumped in their lap, often without having had any say in it, and now has to somehow magically transform your visions into reality. Show that you care about their situation and how you can make their day easier through your design choices. Does that animated button really contribute to a better experience or does it just add complexity and development time?

Don't forget yourself either. With better self-awareness and a bit of self-distance, you'll find it easier to handle all setbacks and let your unbridled ego take a backseat.

title: Hantera negativ kritik
text:
# Handle Negative Criticism

It's so easy to receive positive feedback. Then it's called feedback or praise, not criticism. You get an ego boost and finally some recognition for all the work you've put in. But receiving the emotional slap that negative criticism entails is not as easy. The very concept of "getting criticized" or criticizing has a strong negative ring to it. It becomes an discomfort that you immediately want to push away. I've handled it in three bad ways and one good way. I'll take the bad ones first:

## React with Anger

The first impulse, which I've unfortunately followed far too many times, is to see the criticism (and the critic) as a threat that must be fought. I immediately want to show that the person was wrong and throw out every conceivable argument to prove it. At best, they're factual and structured, at worst they're personal attacks like "but what about you…" The consequence is a fight or at least bad vibes, a damaged relationship, and nothing gets solved.

## Playing Dead

The second way to handle criticism is to do the exact opposite. I lie flat, admit everything, and fall in line with the criticism. I become small, almost invisible, and just let the dark cloud pass. If I just don't object, nothing bad will happen to me. I don't want confrontation or to seem difficult. This was a common scenario early in my career when a more senior person criticized my design. I lacked the security and confidence to see the criticism as a suggestion for improvement rather than a list of mistakes that need fixing. The consequence is that I might change something that was actually good, that I don't stand up for my work, and that my confidence drops even further.

## Hear No Evil

The third technique that I want to advise against is ignoring. Maybe you pretend to listen, nod, say you understand, and then do what you want anyway. My children are black belts in this noble art. On the surface, there seems to be a constructive dialogue—you might even discuss in detail what changes need to be made and why. Then I (the critic) walk away satisfied with my effort and hopeful for a result that never materializes. Consequence. This method is tricky because everything seems peaceful and joyful. But as soon as the bluff is exposed, conflict arises, and if I've been evasive, I now need to come up with a bunch of excuses and explanations.

All three methods have something in common. I, as the one receiving the criticism, learn nothing, avoid no mistakes, and don't grow as a person. There's no good dialogue or synergy with the other party. Especially when it comes to design, where both the result and I as a designer get better from constructive criticism.

So what now? Is there a good way to do it? I'm glad you asked. Here's a way that works for me.

## Disconnect the Emotions

Since (negative) criticism often triggers such strong emotions, it's important to get them under control. A good trick is to imagine that the problem has befallen a friend who is now asking for advice. Then it's much easier to disconnect your ego and the need to defend it.

## Accept, Ask, and Reflect

Don't do anything hasty. Don't answer right away even if the matter is urgent. If the criticism is written in an email, read it several times and try to understand what the other party is after. If you get the criticism in a conversation, buy yourself time by asking follow-up questions and asking for time to think before you can give an answer. Note that you have neither agreed with nor rejected the criticism. You've simply received it as a package with unknown contents that you're going to unpack calmly and carefully.

## Analyze and Structure

Your instinct is to immediately start looking for evidence for your own case and flaws in the other person's argument. But could there be something to what the other person is saying? What's the reason for the criticism and what does the other person want to achieve by presenting it? Is the criticism concrete and factual or is it based on opinions and assumptions? Is it directed at something, at you as a person, or at your behavior? What do you agree with, what do you disagree with, and what lies somewhere in between? It's not about determining who's right or who won.

## The Way Forward

The criticism is a story that needs a resolution. Even if you don't plan to make any changes to your original plan, it's important to communicate why. Maybe there wasn't time for such big changes, or other factors that would make it not work. The main thing is that you show that you've taken the criticism to heart, thought it over, and made a decision based on it. The critic often wants a dialogue, not for everything to be exactly as they envisioned. Make the other person feel heard and half the problem is solved.

This may sound simple but it's incredibly hard. Especially when our minds are made to avoid discomfort, where one negative thing weighs more than ten positive ones. So my final piece of advice is to be proactive. Assume you could be wrong, ask for feedback, and create a dialogue. Suddenly, criticism doesn't feel like a stinging slap but rather a helping hand. Good luck.

title: Bli en bättre designer. Börja  skriva.
text:
# Become a Better Designer. Start Writing.

Seth Godin writes a blog post every day. Impressive, maybe something I should try. But I know myself. Even once a week is a challenge. I'm simply not good at perseverance and long-term investments. Just because something is difficult doesn't mean it's impossible or that I won't try (again). Quite the opposite. I'm going to prove it can be done. If not this time, then next.

To increase my chances, I'm going to give myself some good advice along the way, based on my previous failed attempts.

**Write for yourself.**

Before your fingers even reach the keyboard comes the first mental barrier. "What do I have to say that hasn't already been said by people who are smarter, more experienced, famous, and more articulate than me?" There's certainly no shortage of blogs, podcasts, articles, tweets, videos, streams about everything under the sun. It's almost seen as a virtue to keep quiet and not contribute to the noise. My answer: Don't compare yourself to others. Do it for yourself. Forget about likes and followers. It's nice if someone reads what you write, but the goal is to write what you want to write about, not what gets the most likes.

**Dig where you stand.**

I like the motto: "Start where you are, use what you have, and do all you can." Wherever you are in life, your career, or your mental maturity, you have something to say. You don't need to be an expert, but a strong interest in the subject is well worth recommending. Why else should the reader care if you don't. Are you a beginner in the field? Embrace that perspective. Write how you see things with completely fresh eyes. That's something a senior expert can't recreate.

**Let us follow your journey.**

If you blog regularly (as I hopefully soon will), over time your posts become a map of your thoughts and ideas—where you've been and where you're going. Hopefully you'll think your earlier posts were pretty bad, with opinions you no longer share. Congratulations, you've developed. Also dare to explore areas you don't understand and ask questions you don't have answers to. Just listening to experts with firm opinions gets pretty tiring.

**Everyone sucks at first.**

If you haven't written much before, it will feel stiff at first, take a long time, and frankly be pretty bad. Every first draft is terrible. No panic. Read through what you've written, edit, and rewrite. But don't get stuck. If the post turns out bad, boring, or clunky, invest in the next one. Luckily the pixels in the computer never run out. If you're unsure whether you should take it to the next level, ask for feedback from people you admire. Then it's just a matter of continuing to write. No matter what.

**Make your thoughts concrete.**

It's much easier to decide whether an idea is good or bad if you write it down. It immediately becomes more concrete and won't risk being forgotten. If you develop the idea into a longer text, it's also placed in context with other thoughts and ideas. Suddenly patterns emerge that you wouldn't have thought of otherwise. The idea becomes an insight.

**Writing as a workout.**

By writing regularly, you exercise your verbal ability. You'll express yourself in a more structured and pedagogical way, know what you want to say and how to convey it. Everything you write from emails to reports and documentation will get a real boost. Think of all the tedious and incoherent reports you've been forced to read. Don't contribute to the misery.

**Build a verbal portfolio.**

Good design is as much about the thinking behind the work as the result itself. Thinking about design, discussing it, and critiquing it is equally (if not more) valuable than tinkering with pixels. If you're looking for a job, new assignments, or a step up in your career, a blog, vlog, or podcast is a brilliant way to show what you stand for, how you think, and how you see the big picture.

**Set aside time.**

A fixed time or little sessions here and there? Find what works best for you. Expect it to take time, at least at first. Set a deadline or timeframe so you don't overwork yourself. Prioritize quantity over quality. The best training is the one that actually happens. ;-)

I like the meta of writing about how to get started with writing in order to get started with my own writing. To finish, I'll say good luck to us both and I'm already looking forward to what we're going to accomplish.

title: Tre sätt att fly funktionsfabriken
text:
# Three Ways to Escape the Feature Factory

A digital product must constantly evolve, or it dies—especially now that most are being transformed into services requiring a monthly fee. User expectations are to have access to all existing and upcoming features as long as they're paying. More features equal more value for money.

## The majority of your work is wasted

When you ask users what they want, the answer is usually new features. Products with more features also appear more powerful than those with fewer. They sell better. More features sounds like an obvious win-win and is the most common way to develop a product. In reality, only a fraction of all new features get used. Up to 80% are rarely or never used (according to Pendo 2019). So much work for so little effect.

## More features lead to greater complexity

The problem isn't just that new features go unused. They make the product more complex, harder to navigate and sluggish, while users expect unchanged simplicity and clarity. Instagram, for example, started as a clean social app. With few features and a focus on images, it was simple to understand and easy to use. Take a photo, add a filter, share, like, comment. With more features like stories, reels and shop, the app and number of functions have grown. Logically, you could argue it's become better since you can now do so much more. At the same time, complexity has increased and the mental model has become much more convoluted. The simple question "What should I use this app for?" suddenly became very difficult to answer. If the app were instead a physical product, it would resemble the creation below.

So what's the solution?

Should we stop listening to and acting on customers, users and the market? Of course not. Nobody would buy a new iPhone if it were exactly the same as the previous model. We can't just brake development. Here are three paths to developing your product in a more constructive way.

## 1. Needs before features

Every new product starts by solving a problem better, more efficiently, or more simply than existing solutions. This is the very reason the product exists, its USP or "Jobs to be done". Unfortunately, many products lose focus along the way and the original purpose gets buried under an ever-growing pile of new features. The simple becomes complex, the quick becomes cumbersome.

Instead of asking which features are in demand, focus on how we can help users achieve their goals better or faster. We also need to keep track of users' actual needs—whether they change or if new ones emerge.

Questions to ask:
- What are the main needs the product satisfies?
- How can we make the product do this as well as possible?
- How do we ensure the product remains relevant in the future?

## 2. Structure

There are ways to make a large and complex product feel accessible. The trick is not to overload the user's cognitive capacity. Logical structure, text and naming that are easy to understand and remember, consistent UX, and above all, hiding features that aren't needed right now. Photoshop lets me switch workspaces with tools tailored to the task I'm working on at the moment. Photo editing has one set of tools, illustration has another. I can also create custom workspaces suited to my specific needs. Everything else is hidden, but available if I need it.

Structure can also mean breaking up a product that has become too large, like when Messenger and Facebook became two separate apps. Another example is Photoshop, which couldn't meet the new needs of UX designers. Adobe chose to create XD from scratch—a much simpler and faster tool focused entirely on creating prototypes and flows.

In some cases, the path forward is the opposite: merging or integrating products. Think of all the people juggling multiple systems, having to report in one system and then copy and paste everything into another. Here you want a system that handles everything, or systems that talk to each other. Users shouldn't have to pay the price for poor IT solutions.

Questions to ask:
- Are there major differences in how your product is used by different roles, purposes or tasks?
- Can we split up or structure the product in a way that makes it easier for users to understand and use?
- Can we streamline users' daily work by reducing duplicate work, multiple logins and the number of systems used?

## 3. Work with what you have

Your product is only as good as the value it creates for users. In other words, the better and more efficiently your product is used, the more value it creates. If users learn to use your product's existing features in better ways, the product automatically becomes more valuable. Best of all, you didn't have to build a single new feature. Magic. Note that this isn't about increasing the amount of time users spend in your app. It's about what's best for users, not your shareholders.

Another approach is to update the experience. The same old product can feel brand new with a bit of visual refresh. Small improvements might be enough—creating consistency in colors, language and UX patterns. Clear out all the hundred shades of gray that have crept into the product and make sure the language is consistent and on brand. Build a design system if you don't already have one.

The language app Duolingo redesigned its appearance without changing the content or functionality. The result is still a completely new experience.

Questions to ask:
- How can we help users use our product even better?
- Can we make our product more attractive, clear or accessible through copy and UI?
- How do we ensure the experience remains consistent over time and is reflected throughout the product?

## Finally, choose your features carefully

New features aren't evil—they're what drives development forward. Just don't let them be the only way to solve every problem. Users don't want more buttons (no matter what they say). They want better ways to solve their problems. Make sure that's what you're building instead of another feature nobody will use.

title: Jag har precis lurat 40 pers och kommit undan med det.
text:
I just tricked 40 people and got away with it.

For 16 weeks, I've deceived a large group of people without being discovered. They were even happy and grateful. Best of all, I even got paid for the trouble.

That's how it felt when I finished the course at Nackademin this week that I've been teaching. A mixture of pride, relief, and amazement. Completely unbelievable that it worked out, without anyone exposing (or even suspecting) that I was just pretending to be a teacher, that I actually don't know what I'm talking about, that I lack relevant education, solid experience, haven't done any of my own research or contributed any original insights. That no one noticed I just cobbled together a bunch of stuff I found on the internet and threw it together in a PowerPoint. No one stood up and said "the emperor has no clothes." (At least I had underwear on below when I was zooming.)

But all the imposter syndrome disappeared when I got to see all the final presentations and realized they were incredibly impressive. The students didn't just listen and understand what I was trying to tell them in my ramblings, but also applied everything correctly, found new insights, dared to scrap ideas that didn't work, tested, brainstormed, reasoned, sketched, prototyped, and came up with neat, innovative concepts.

Then I felt like a proud parent watching their children graduate. Can you believe how fast they grow.

I'm really glad I managed to ignore that voice that said "who do you think you are, you don't know anything about design thinking or user-centered design." Because even though the voice was partly right (I know significantly more about both now than before the course), it was wrong on one important point. I AM exactly the right person for the job. Just watch me.

title: Det stora penistestet som varje designer bör göra.
text:
# The Great Penis Test Every Designer Should Do

Created on 2021-01-27 10:19
Published on 2021-01-27 13:34

Let's face it. We humans have dirty minds. We react to anything that alludes to sex and even when there's nothing there, we're happy to create an association. Elongated things become penises, round and curved objects transform into breasts and buttocks 🍆 🍑. This has been used in art and advertising since time immemorial. From the sublime to the playful (like the example below), to the provocative to the vulgar or in poor taste (unfortunately far too many examples). Our (dirty) minds don't need much to get going.

The tricky part is when you do it unintentionally. Especially when it comes to logos, symbols or icons where there's room for interpretation. As a designer, you're always trying to find a new angle, make the logo unique and exciting. You modify, tweak, rotate, add and subtract. You're so deep in your design that you become blind to the obvious. That's when, my friend, it's time for the Penis Test. You don't need a ruler for this, you don't even need to have a penis. It's inclusive and pressure-free.

Start with a simple (but oh so important) question. Does your logo look like a penis? (or something else equivocal)

Answer: No.

Great, you can continue the test.

Answer: Yes.

Is it intended to look like or allude to a penis? If so, you've succeeded—your logo is done. However, if it's not supposed to look like a penis but does anyway, it's best to scrap the proposal and start over.

**Explore all angles.**

Your logo will be seen from every direction and angle. Make sure it holds up when rotated, inverted, or turned into a pattern. The Office of Government Commerce (OGC) inadvertently becomes something entirely different when rotated.

**Negative space.**

You're not just designing the shape itself but also the spaces in between. If you're smart and skillful, you can exploit this, as in the classic FedEx logo (the arrow between the E and x). Otherwise, it can have unforeseen consequences.

**Typography.**

Choice of typeface, spacing between letters and words affects not only readability but also the word's visual appearance. Innocent words suddenly take on a completely different meaning. Even pairing lowercase c, d, and b can create a problematic word image. A lowercase l easily becomes a capital I. No problem if you're not simultaneously turning a letter into a heart. (Thanks Locum for this unforgettable and world-famous example.)

**Figures.**

Is the logo supposed to represent a person or a creature? Make sure the figure doesn't have any objects near its crotch. Should multiple figures interact? Examine carefully that this interaction cannot be misinterpreted. Haven't you designed a figure but it appears anyway? It can be a funny Easter egg as long as the figure isn't doing anything inappropriate, as was the case with A Style Clothing.

**Name.**

It's almost an unwritten rule that no matter what word you choose, it means something obscene in some language or slang. With today's digital presence, there's a good chance people from other countries will come into contact with your product. Here, Google is your best friend.

Good luck. May the schwartz be with you.

title: Vart tog all (B2C) kärlek vägen?
text:
# Where Did All the (B2C) Love Go?

In the beginning, everything is always so beautiful and romantic. I'm constantly courted, I feel seen and understood, sweet promises are whispered in my ear. "Come to us, we'll solve all your problems, we promise. We're here for you, today, tomorrow, and forever." In the end, I fall for the seduction, hoping that this time it will actually work.

But as soon as I sign the contract, something happens. Or rather, that's precisely what doesn't happen. Nothing happens. All those expectations, the magic I was promised in the advertisements, the enticing words from the salesman's lips. It all comes to nothing. Just silence and emptiness. Maybe the occasional anonymous monthly letter, advertisements that have nothing to do with me, or a time-consuming and tedious survey that gives nothing back.

Why does it always turn out this way? Is it really impossible for a company to have a meaningful relationship with its customers? I understand that I'm not the only one, of course they can see others, but I still want to feel seen and special. I want them to remember my choices, preferences, and history. I want them to reward me for my loyalty, to discover and fix things that don't work or could be improved on their own. If I never use all my data or my bandwidth, why not give me a nudge and offer me something that better suits my needs? And ALL the banks out there. Why, why, why don't I get notified when there's insufficient coverage on my account before a payment is due? When I then have to pay that fat reminder fee to CSN, I'm just as angry at myself as I am at you. Why didn't you say anything when you knew? Why did you just look the other way and think it's not your responsibility? I feel deceived, I gave you my trust and you betray it again and again.

Then there's the matter of breaking up in a nice way. It shouldn't come as a shock that I've found someone else after being completely ignored for several years. The fact that you suddenly show up with a special offer I don't want to miss just feels desperate and calculated. Do you really want to show that it's a shame? Ask me why I'm choosing to switch, what you could do better, what I was missing, or what the competitor did better? All this text message shows is that loyalty doesn't pay off—I'm only rewarded at the beginning of the relationship and when I cancel. It feels like a very dysfunctional relationship where one party has to threaten to break up as soon as something doesn't work.

It doesn't have to be that damn difficult. You have all my information, you know where my mailbox is, what I've bought, what I like and don't like. Grocery stores should understand from my purchase history that I'm vegetarian and probably don't appreciate ads with dead animals on sale. I don't want to choose from the left menu between vegetarian or organic. I want both. I don't care if it doesn't work with your platform or that the target demographic "eco-vegetarian" is missing from your user profiles. Get it right.

It's time for companies to take relationship-building seriously. Not with consumption-driven tempting prices, misdirected loyalty programs, or flashy advertising campaigns. If you want my loyalty, my data, and my money, you need to know who I am, what I want and need. Only then can our relationship live happily ever after.

title: Bli en bättre designer med DISC
text:
# Become a Better Designer with DISC

Created on 2020-04-30 10:46
Published on 2020-04-30 11:12

To become a truly good designer requires more than creative ideas and nice sketches. The real challenge lies in communicating your work in the right way and selling it to your team, colleagues, or clients. Otherwise, your design remains nothing but sketches on your laptop.

A classic mistake I made as a junior designer was believing that good design speaks for itself. Once the sketches were done, I thought I was finished. Every question I interpreted as criticism and proof that the others didn't understand anything about design. It turned out that I was the one who didn't understand anything about communication. Now, a bit older and wiser, I've found new tools to design communication itself. With a small twist, the popular DISC model can ensure that your ideas really get through.

## What is DISC?

At its core, DISC is a four-quadrant model that describes four different personality traits. Usually, a person has a combination of two quadrants where one is somewhat more prominent. This gives you 16 different personality types and adds more depth to the analysis. The model shows how the different traits interact and complement each other. In other words, the opposite sides mirror opposing personality traits.

Here are three areas where DISC can help you become a better designer and communicator.

## Get to Know Yourself

It's difficult to communicate with others if you don't get to know yourself first. What are my strengths and weaknesses, what comes easily and what's hard, what's fun and what's really tedious? By writing it down, you make it extra clear and certain patterns start to emerge. Then I can plan ahead and compensate for upcoming difficulties. Personally, I know I have strong yellow influencing tendencies. Anything involving creativity, big visions, or workshops, I handle without problems. But if I have to sit alone with monotonous tasks requiring careful planning and many details, then I'm going to struggle. If I know what's coming, I can prepare myself properly and make sure I reach the goal.

## Get to Know Others

Why is it so easy to collaborate with some people and almost impossible with others? My big mistake was assuming that others were just like me. What was important to me should be equally important to them. I expected to be met with the same enthusiasm, openness, and energy that I gave. When I didn't get it, I felt hurt, disappointed, and thought the other person was uncommitted or downright unfriendly.

Here it's about leaving your ego at home. Respect and welcome differences just as much as common ground. You rarely get new perspectives from someone who thinks and feels exactly like you do. Understand what matters to the other party. If you want to convince an analytical person, you won't get far with spontaneous creativity and gut feelings. Here you need to show that you have control of the situation and have thought things through carefully. Instead of mood boards, show documentation with hard facts, research, and statistics. Pure opinion without evidence is completely irrelevant.

## The Whole Cost Circle in the Project

There's no part that's better or worse—they complement each other in an excellent way. Make sure you also think from these different perspectives. Focus on areas that are important to the person you're talking to. Here are some example questions from each color group.

**The red part focuses on action and results.**
- What's the timeline and deadlines? What's the next step, when should it be done, etc.?
- What roles exist in the project and who's responsible for what?
- What results are we expected to achieve? What's the project expected to deliver, improve, optimize, etc.?

**The yellow part is about creativity and inspiration.**
- What's innovative in the project? What's the creative peak, the power of innovation, etc.?
- What's the big vision? What's become better when the project is complete?
- Why should I, the customer, the people care?
- How will we communicate and package our message?

**The green part is the thoughtful, human, and benefit-oriented.**
- How does the project relate to sustainability, environment, ethics, etc.?
- How will the project make the world, society better or people happier and more productive?
- How will we work together as a team?

**The blue part concerns the logical and analytical.**
- What evidence do we have for our decisions?
- What measurable goals do we have and how will we measure if we've succeeded?
- Do we have the resources (time, money, personnel, competence, space, contacts, knowledge, experience, authority, muscle, etc.) required to succeed?
- What are the risks and costs?

The DISC model is simple to understand and use as a starting point. But remember that a model is just a model. Neither you, your colleagues, nor your customers ARE yellow or red. People don't behave the same way in every situation; they're not static but constantly developing. Reality is, fortunately, much more complex and exciting than a colorful ball.

Good luck with your next project.

title: 3. The Promised Land That Wasn't
text:
3. The Promised Land That Wasn't
3. The Promised Land That Wasn't
Created on 2026-04-24 11:17
Published on 2026-04-24 11:23
I used to tell people UX design was a good career bet.
Good money. Remote-friendly. Creative but structured. No computer science degree required. Lot of job opportunities. For a while, the market rewarded that pitch. New UX educations, bootcamps and online courses popped up all over the place. The promised land got crowded fast.
A market that built more supply than it could absorb
Junior designers competed with mid-level candidates competed with seniors who had been laid off competed with career changers who had finished a bootcamp three months earlier. The pool of designers tripled while the amount of jobs stayed the same.
Naturally companies raised the bar. Eight years required experience, earlier the max, now became the floor. Just to keep the volume down.
As one recruiter said:
"If you write five years in the ad, you get applicants with three. Write eight, you get applicants with five. The numbers have lost meaning. It is an arms race with job requirements."
A.I. enters the stage
Design and creativity were seen as the only thing machines couldn't do. Until they did.
While established designers spent months debating whether AI was worth using, the newcomers embraced it immediately. AI let them punch above their weight with minimal effort. Generating visual concepts, producing wireframes that look polished, writing synthetic UX copy, producing case study text from rough notes — all became faster and cheaper.
The surface layer is what most hiring processes actually evaluate. A portfolio that looks sharp can now be produced by someone with three months of experience and the right prompts. The signal that hiring managers already struggled to read got noisier at exactly the moment the supply of designers peaked.
The portfolio that used to be a strong signal about your skills and thinking could now be fabricated and generated. Spotting the difference between something real — with genuine afterthoughts and real-world compromises — versus a well-generated portfolio, became really hard.
Only in later stages, when you actually need to present your cases, describe your why, all the failed attempts, twists and turns during the project, only then does it become obvious if there is something behind the shiny artifacts.
This problem is not hard — it is wicked.
A hard problem has a solution if you work at it long enough. A wicked problem is one where every attempted fix shifts the burden somewhere else and creates a new problem downstream. It is when a system is part of other systems, where problems and solutions are intertwined.
There is no single solution. Especially when there are other factors that nobody can foresee or control — wars, economic tendencies, the speed at which AI can help or replace designers, companies taking safe bets, replacing workers with AI, or stopping hiring juniors.
When looking backward, it all kind of makes sense: Good times when everybody needed designers led to schools and bootcamps rushing to fill the gap, led to a flooded market and companies raising the bar, candidates using AI to get through, requiring hiring managers to do the same to filter the avalanche of applications.
And here we are.
What the research showed
What surprised me the most from all the conversations was the agreement. Both sides described the same broken experience from opposite ends. Both want fewer, better interactions. Both feel trapped in a system they did not design and cannot unilaterally change.
The system optimizes for activity. Volume. Throughput. Not for matches.
AI is used as a crutch by both parties, not as a constructive tool that brings out the best on both sides. Both sides use it, but not well. More AI is the answer to nobody. Nor are more tests, more steps in the process, or harder requirements.
We need to find a way to make the process more transparent and more human. Leave the administration, tables, and numbers to the machines and focus on conversations and relations. We need to stop treating the hiring process as finding the right set of skills to fix a problem. Instead, treat it as the start of a relationship where a person joins a group with the intent to create something amazing together.
That gap between what the system rewards and what both sides actually want is the design problem hiding inside the market problem.
This is the third and the last in my series about the hiring process.
Part 1:
https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/we-stuck-ai-loop-boris-kehr-kfzdf/
Part 2:
https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/part-2-everyone-knows-its-broken-nobody-owns-fix-boris-kehr-x0e9f/
This series comes from six weeks of interviews — 24 conversations with designers looking for work, recruiters trying to hire them, and hiring managers caught in the middle. I'm sharing what I learned because I believe both sides deserve a better process. One built on transparency, dignity, and actual human contact.
The final report:
https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7454466805110104064/

title: Bad UX made me do it
text:
Bad UX made me do it
Bad UX made me do it
Created on 2026-04-29 16:15
Published on 2026-04-29 16:23
Last year I spent almost a full year trying to find a new job.
I thought it would be straightforward. Twenty years of experience, a decent network, solid portfolio. I was completely wrong. I got ghosted more times than I could count. Hit walls I could not see. And somewhere along the way I stopped feeling like a senior professional looking for a new opportunity rather like an irrelevant has-been that nobody needed.
Rage against the machine
The anger came next. I needed someone to blame. The recruiter. The algorithm. The job market. The AI. None of it stuck, because none of it was quite right. Eventually I got tired of being angry and decided to switch focus. I wrote about it and it became one of my most recognized things I have ever published on LinkedIn. Apparently a lot of people were sitting with the same feeling and just needed someone to say it out loud.
Stop chasing waterfalls
I stopped chasing design jobs and enrolled in AI and machine learning instead. Suddenly I had purpose and agency. Instead of writing meaningless application I wrote code, learned the math and principles behind what we lazily call AI. I learned more in a few months than for the past years.
Don’t look back in anger
A year later, with less desperation and more curiosity, I went back in. Two months of interviews with 20+ people across all sides of the hiring process. Trying to find the shape of the thing and understand how all parts fit together.
Wicked games
Hiring is a wicked problem. And what is happening to designers in this market is not one thing going wrong. It is many things colliding: the economics of design work shifting, AI rewriting what the job actually is, companies pausing on juniors, then seniors, then everyone while they figure out what a design team even looks like now. Wars, recessions, a post-pandemic hangover, organizations rethinking everything from the ground up. None of those things in isolation explains what happened to me or to the people I interviewed. All of them together do.
What I did not expect to find was hope.
Designers getting creative about how they show their value. Staying visible, building things, refusing to disappear quietly. Hiring managers pushing back against full automation, keeping humans in the decision loop, delegating the administration to the machine instead of the judgment. Small moves, but the right ones.
Here comes the sun
I do not know if this report fixes anything. That was never the goal. The goal was to understand it well enough to stop feeling like it was just me, and maybe to make the conversation a little more honest on both sides. That felt worth two months of unpaid work on a Sunday afternoon.
Thank you to everyone who talked to me. You are the report.
If you haven't yet, read the full Ghosted report at
designloop.se

title: Are we stuck in an AI loop, AI loop, AI loop?
text:
Are we stuck in an AI loop, AI loop, AI loop?
Are we stuck in an AI loop, AI loop, AI loop?
Created on 2026-04-21 09:14
Published on 2026-04-21 09:44
Part 1.
Candidates using AI to write cover letters, picking out the right keywords, hoping that just the pure amount of applications sent will help them land the job they want. Nobody really knows what it takes to pass the first sorting. They try out everything just to see what sticks. Even if all criteria are met, there is no guarantee that your application will make it to the next step. The bar is invisible, and the feedback signal is almost always absent. What's left is just to try out everything and hope for the best. It feels more like a lottery than a system that promotes merit.
Many candidates I've spoken to don't even want to use AI because they feel their voice is getting lost. At the same time, they don't want to be left behind when they suspect everyone else is using AI and getting an upper hand. They suspect recruiters are using AI for screening too, making it impossible to know who they're actually talking to. Is it a human or a machine? In the worst cases, both sides show up performing — the candidate rehearsing answers, the recruiter reading AI-generated questions from a screen.
What we get is candidates creating an abundance of applications just to hope to get through, making recruiters use AI to sort out the vast majority on unclear premises. Everyone is worse off. Neither side can stop.
When applying for jobs was cumbersome and hard, and writing a good personal letter took real effort, it was a sign of dedication. A personal letter could actually tell quite a lot about who you are and whether you are a good fit for the role. Now this signal has disappeared. Those who use AI carelessly lose their personality in the process. Those who still write by hand can't be trusted either — everything is suspected to be AI slop and has therefore lost its value. Meanwhile, the recruiters who are actually making hires have mostly abandoned the formal system altogether. They're working their personal networks instead.
The system gets overwhelmed by noise. The good is mixed with the bad, sorting it out takes enormous effort, and the personal skills that actually matter most are the hardest to communicate. Ghost has become so normal that one candidate I interviewed stopped categorizing it — no tag, no color code, just silence filed away as expected.
Everyone feels stuck in this hamster wheel. Candidates are busy trying to survive, find the job, and just keep afloat. Hiring managers are swamped. They don't have time or the mandate to recreate the whole system. They don't like it, but they don't see any other way. That's the most common thing I heard — on both sides.
Both sides are using AI to survive a system they wish didn't exist. It's like watching people build better weapons to fight in a war nobody wants.
I'm not anti-AI. I use it constantly. But I'm watching two communities optimize their way into mutual distrust, and it's hard to see the endgame.
The AI arms race in hiring isn't a technical problem. It's a design problem. The system incentivizes the wrong behavior. Until that changes, more AI will just mean better gaming, not better matches.
This series comes from six weeks of interviews — 22 conversations with designers looking for work, recruiters trying to hire them, and hiring managers caught in the middle. I'm sharing what I learned because I believe both sides deserve a better process. One built on transparency, dignity, and actual human contact.

title: Små ord som skapa stor skillnad
text:
# Small Words That Make a Big Difference

Small Words That Make a Big Difference
Created on 2021-05-05 10:26
Published on 2021-05-05 10:53

One of the most important phases at the beginning of a project is defining the problem, setting clear goals, and writing a "why." Nothing complicated—if you want a successful project, everyone needs to know what they should do and why. But what people often don't think about is how much impact word choice and wording have. Imagine we're starting an important project that's supposed to get our company or product back on track. Here are different ways to describe the initiative:

We're going to crush our competitors and seize their customers.
We're going to outrun our competitors and win over their customers.
We're going to outshine our competitors and attract their customers.

Do we call our meeting room a war room, planning room, inspiration room, or play room? Should we prevent users from making mistakes or should we make it easier for them to do things right?

It might seem trivial to weigh every word when they mean roughly the same thing. Everyone understands anyway... But it's not about understanding the meaning. The same basic meaning but different tonality sends thoughts in completely different directions and also affects which solutions we choose. Design work doesn't begin in Sketch; it begins with choosing the right words.

It's important to find a tonality that fits you, your culture, and your users. If you're a bunch of hungry stockbrokers, maybe you should crush all resistance and own your opponents. But this vocabulary probably won't work as well for a group of preschool teachers.

Is your day filled with a bunch of musts? Try replacing the word "must" with something else.

"I get to fold the laundry, I have the honor of picking up the children, it'll be inspiring to go grocery shopping, I'm looking forward to cleaning the garage."

I know, it's a bit silly. You still have to do the boring stuff. But just like the exercise "yes and..." instead of "yes but..." a positively charged word creates a completely different energy and momentum. Try it yourself.

More on "Yes but, yes and..." https://www.metodbanken.se/post/ja-men-ja-och

title: Nyckeln till bra feedback-kultur
text:
# The Key to Good Feedback Culture

The Key to Good Feedback Culture
Created on 2021-03-02 10:38
Published on 2021-03-02 11:23

During my time in school, feedback was part of everyday life. Especially in design education, it was a given that your work would be scrutinized and questioned. It was painful to see many late nights of hard work being questioned and picked apart. I had a strong urge to throw everything into a burning wastebasket. After a few rounds, I managed to get over the negative feelings and realized that the design actually improved after a grueling critique. My way of thinking critically improved, and I found many flaws before the design was put to the test. I could no longer gloss over a bad idea with some flashy design and a cool typeface.

Judge my surprise when I realized that in working life, good feedback is a rarity. All reflection was saved for the annual development conversation with nonexistent follow-up. As employees (designers), we're expected to be creative, innovative, high-performing, and productive. We should think outside the box, learn new things and skills, but without making any (costly) mistakes. All of this without receiving continuous, constructive feedback.

Why is this feedback thing so difficult? Both giving it, receiving it, asking for it, and offering it. Is it the feeling of being criticized, having your competence questioned, or the fear of being perceived as a pessimistic complainer? There are many who would rather drive a project into the ditch convinced they're right than have their opinions questioned in time.

Planting a Seed

How do you, as an individual, reverse a feedback-skeptical organization? The best way is to start gently and ask for feedback yourself. Don't use the word criticism, not even constructive criticism. Even the word feedback can be very loaded and cause people to slowly back out of the room. Package the feedback session as improvement suggestions, a sounding board, finding problems, iteration, or some other meaningless buzzword. Say that you've gotten stuck with an idea, sketch, or prototype and need help moving forward. It's good to have a few concrete questions up your sleeve to steer the conversation in the right direction. For example, "How can I make the design feel more pedagogical?" or "Is there anything in the design that feels unclear to you?"

Repeat this procedure until it feels completely natural for both you and your colleagues. Don't forget to thank people for feedback even if you disagree or don't plan to make the suggested changes. Hopefully, you've planted a seed for a flourishing feedback culture. Now you can carefully offer your help when your colleagues get stuck (in other words, need feedback).

Prerequisites for a Constructive Feedback Culture

Framework:
Set the right level for how you want feedback to function. How and when you give it, what it should contain, what tone it needs to have, whether there's a moderator, and so on. With a framework in place, you don't need to find it tricky or awkward—you just follow the protocol. It's good to have a battery of the most common questions so it's easy to get the session started.

Safety:
It can be very difficult to receive feedback, even if it's constructive and well-formulated. As a recipient, I need to feel safe and know that the feedback is honest but comes with good intentions and aims to help. Time and place are also important—preferably a one-on-one or in a closed setting where what's said doesn't spread.

Trust:
Who gives the feedback is at least as important as what is said. Is it some guy on Flashback or is it your closest friend? You can still listen to both, but the probability is higher that you'll take feedback to heart from someone you trust, look up to, and respect.

Routine:
Feedback is like exercise. It gets easier and better the more often you do it. Better short and frequent than long and seldom. Get feedback as a natural part of every partial delivery, check-in, or presentation, and it becomes easier to see the positive effects of feedback.

What feedback culture exists at your company or organization? Is there anyone actively working on this issue?

If you have any opinions on this article, I now (these days) gratefully welcome all forms of feedback. ;-)

title: Part 2: Everyone knows it's broken. Nobody owns the fix.
text:
Part 2: Everyone knows it's broken. Nobody owns the fix.
Part 2: Everyone knows it's broken. Nobody owns the fix.
Created on 2026-04-22 15:49
Published on 2026-04-22 15:59
There is no single villain here. No one bad policy, no one bad decision. The hiring system stays broken partly because the pain isn't distributed evenly.
The failure lands in different places
For candidates, it fails loudly. Silence where a decision should be. Rejection templates that say nothing. An invisible bar that keeps moving. For companies, it fails softly. When great candidates fall through the cracks, that loss rarely gets attributed to "the hiring interface." It gets filed under the market, salary expectations, employer brand, senior talent shortage. Problems that live somewhere else, owned by someone else. The people with the power to redesign the process rarely feel the urgency to do so.
Volume looks like opportunity until it becomes noise
More applicants sounds like a feature. More choice should mean better matching. But when 200 to 500 people apply fast, many with AI assistance, hiring stops being evaluation and becomes throughput. Criteria quietly shift: job titles, years of experience, brand-name companies, keyword matches. Shallow filters aren't what anyone wants. They're what scale demands.
The job spec is often the first thing that breaks
Several of the recruiters I interviewed told me a version of the same blunt thing: the job ad is frequently a weak artifact of an unfinished internal conversation. The role isn't defined as a business outcome. The problem isn't scoped. Tradeoffs aren't agreed on. So recruiting begins on a moving target, and once the process starts, requirements shift mid-funnel. From the outside, this reads as randomness. From the inside, it's uncertainty being managed in public. More applicants don't help here. If you don't know what good looks like, more options just mean more confusion.
When everything is AI-written, nothing is a signal
Once people believe the first filter is automated, the rational move is to write for the machine. Candidates tune CVs for ATS. They generate cover letters that "sound right", filling in all the keywords just to pass the filters. Recruiters get texts that feel generic and start distrusting all of it. Both sides become more defensive: candidates apply more because the feedback loop is dead, recruiters filter harder because the pile is unmanageable. The process becomes a conversation between tools, with humans doing cleanup on both ends.
Nobody has the mandate to stop the machine
Candidates can't stop. People are paying rent and trying to keep momentum. Recruiters are screaming, not screening. Hiring managers feel the cost of bad hires but don't own the tooling. Leadership optimizes for speed and risk reduction. Even when everyone agrees it's broken, the system keeps running because stopping it isn't an option anyone has.
The platforms profit from this without being the villain. More applications looks like growth. More engagement looks like success. When hiring gets harder, the platform becomes more necessary. The infrastructure does what it's rewarded for: activity, not accuracy.
I'm not anti-AI. I use it constantly. But we've built a system where AI makes the judgments and humans do the waiting. That's backwards. The fix isn't less AI. It's a different interface between people. One that rewards context over keywords.
This is the part two in my series about the hiring process.
Read the first part here:
https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/we-stuck-ai-loop-boris-kehr-kfzdf/
Part 3:
https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/3-promised-land-wasnt-boris-kehr-jtkof/
This series comes from six weeks of interviews — 24 conversations with designers looking for work, recruiters trying to hire them, and hiring managers caught in the middle. I'm sharing what I learned because I believe both sides deserve a better process. One built on transparency, dignity, and actual human contact.

https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7454466805110104064/

title: Vilken design är bäst?
text:
# Which Design is Better?

Which is better? It's a question that often pops up on various design forums online. A couple of fairly similar design proposals sit side by side and people are asked to vote for their favorite. Nothing strange about that, you might think. It's surely a great way to gather feedback.

Let's start by looking at how you can even answer such a question? How do I know what's good or bad? There are two ways to think about it.

## The Objective Rulebook

Here you start from a universal standard to assess the design's qualities. The better the design has followed these rules, the more points it gets on the scale. There are tons of rules for typography, layout, color theory, graphic form, photography, communication and usability. But which of these scales should you follow? A high score in one can be really low in another. Rules are there to be challenged and broken. How should I know if the designer broke them out of ignorance or intentionally to create a certain effect?

## The Subjective Gut Feeling

Design's superpower is that it speaks to our emotions—whether we like something or not, a reaction occurs. Design reaches our subconscious, our experiences and preferences. But if the gut feeling is subjective, then it's always right, isn't it? No one can question that my feelings aren't valid. If I don't like pink, there's a good chance I won't like your pink design. Does that make the design bad then? But if someone else loves pink? Does the design suddenly become good again? Then the question isn't about which design is better, but which preferences are most common.

The purpose of design can also be to create negative emotions or to provoke—for example, an image of a starving polar bear. Can design be good even if I don't like it or how it makes me feel? Will I be able to separate my feelings from the design's quality?

What is considered stylish and beautiful also varies over time. Today's hottest trend is tomorrow's biggest flop. Does the design's value and quality decrease over time then? Or is the design still good even if it's not as sought after?

## Tell Me What You Want, What You Really, Really Want

Another problem is the result of the question itself. There's no consensus on how those surveyed answer the question. Are they marketers, typographers, or UX designers? Are they basing their judgment on gut feeling or ten years of experience? Are they cautious or brutally honest? When you then mix all the answers and perspectives, the result becomes both difficult to interpret and misleading.

When and who you ask also matters. One day 56 people like version A and 67 liked B. The next day 82 liked A but only 49 liked B. What does that mean? Who should you trust?

Just because one option is better than the other doesn't mean either of them is good. Both might be completely worthless and will miss the target regardless of the voting result.

## Context is King

Judging design without any context, background, or purpose is both difficult and pointless. Just because one design appeals to me more doesn't mean it will resonate with the intended target audience. Is there even an intended target audience? Put skatteverket.se next to Nike.com. Everyone can say which one is cooler or more entertaining. But does that automatically mean Nike.com is better? Why not take the cool design and repaint the tax agency with celebrities and attractive models? Or is it the case that both are good at what they're supposed to do, and design is just a way for them to do their job?

In other words, design is subordinate to purpose, and without knowing the purpose, you can't assess how good the design is. The tax agency can be stiff and boring but still be excellently designed. Cool doesn't automatically mean good. Say what?

Design without a purpose is just an empty shell, a gift wrapper. It becomes a template waiting to be placed in the right context. But if you as a designer start with a clear purpose, target audience, problem you're supposed to solve, and needs to be met, then there's an excellent way to know which design is better. Test it on real users and you won't have to ask the audience.

title: What I learned from getting fired
text:
What I learned from getting fired
What I learned from getting fired
Created on 2023-06-14 13:46
Published on 2023-06-14 14:10
Getting fired sucks. Big time. I should know, after getting fired three times just this year. Beat that if you can. Or rather don’t. When life gives you hard lessons you better listen. Here are some of my insights:
How to avoid getting fired
Do your research.
Before accepting a job, look how the company is doing economically. How is their product doing? Any recent layoffs? How do they make many and how dos the market look like in the near future?
Ask the hard questions.
During your interview, ask about the profitability of the company. How are they handling the downturn? Why are they hiring right now? If you are going to invest your time in that company, make sure it’s a good investment.
Read the sings.
Nowadays nobody is safe, no matter how long you have worked there or where you are in the hierarchy. Keep your eyes and ears open. How is the company doing, what are the competitors doing, what is AI doing. Often when you backtrack from you being fired, the sings were there, you were just not paying attention.
Know the "rules"
“What does someone have to do to get fired around here?” is a great question. Will questioning your boss get you promoted or fired? Quickly find out the (often unwritten) rules. Observe and ask. When doing something risky, you should know about it, and be ready to take the consequences.
Read the agreement carefully BEFORE signing it.
Yes it is boring and hard to understand, but take your time. If you are unsure, let someone that know this stuff take a look. When / if shit hits the fan you know what has been agreed.
Be a prepper.
You don’t need a bunker, but you should be prepared. Winter is coming. The best time to get ready for bad times is during the good times. Save up money, grow your network, join a union, have a plan B. Just in case.
What to do when you got fired
Don’t hold a grudge.
Employment is a kind of relationship. It can ended by both part for different reasons. The main thing is to end it well. Be sad, be hurt, but also be empathic. Your manager don’t hate you, he or she probably didn’t want this either. Keeping a good relationship may hep you to get a new job or a good referral.
Perspective and acceptance.
People get fired all the time. It is not the end of the world, and probably not even your fault. There was probably nothing you could have differently that would change the fact. Use your energi to focus on the future.
Take the opportunity.
Maybe you can keep your old computer or get an extra month payment. Don’t beg, but discuss. Your manager is (most often) a decent person that wants to help out.
Check with an external part.
Are you a member of a union, give them a call and see if everything is done by the book.
Take your time.
Remember all the things you wanted do but didn’t have time. Now it’s your chance. Write a book, travel, play Fortnite. If you are short on money find the cheap or the free stuff. Hang out with your friends, pets or family.
Reflect.
Before jumping on the next job take this opportunity to reflect. Do you want a new job? Or do you want to study? Maybe start your own business. Questions that has been on hold for a long time. What is making you happy? What do you really want to do?
Take care of yourself.
Being unemployed is actually a (shitty) job, very stressful with no payment. Only thing you get is loans and a bad self-esteem. Don’t forget to take care of yourself. Mindfulness, exercise, good food and support from friends and family will help.
Experiment and learn.
If the old way ot a new job don't work, try something else. Do a video instead of that booooring CV and cover letter. Talk to new people. Learn new things. The life is your oyster.
Be positive.
Things will work out sooner or later. I promise.
I’m going into a long hot summer with no idea of what to next. 50% scared and 50% excited. Everything is possible. This could be a beginning of a beautiful future. See you on the other side.

title: Vad gör en Product Manager hela dagarna?
text:
# What Does a Product Manager Do All Day?

What Does a Product Manager Do All Day?

Created on 2021-10-06 15:13
Published on 2021-10-07 09:02

If you work in any capacity with a digital product, a Product Manager is someone you really need to keep a close eye on. They're the ones pointing out the future of the product and, by extension, your future too. But how does a PM know where to go and how to get there? I'm asking Nina Holtz, PM at Funnel, and Viktor Löfgren, PM at WOC.

Not many people know what a Product Manager does. Do you have a good elevator pitch you usually pull out for outsiders?

**Nina:**

I usually say that together with developers and designers, I make sure we prioritize and build the right things in the product. I mainly think about the customer and business perspective, the designers focus on user experience, and the developers focus on technical solutions.

**Viktor:**

I think it depends a lot on the type of company, but it's about working with expectations. Expectations in terms of developing a product in a certain direction, understanding your customers' and users' expectations and needs, managing business and market conditions. Being the glue between users/customers and other stakeholders. I'm often the interpreter who speaks all three "languages"—technology, business, and market.

How did you become a PM? Was it a coincidence, a necessity, or a career goal?

**Nina:**

It was maybe a bit of everything. I was there when Funnel started, but I worked in the Customer Success team. In the beginning when we were so few, it was natural to work closely with the development team. I attended stand-ups and was in many discussions about what we should build next. As we grew, that happened less often and I missed working closely with that part of the company. Plus, I thought the PM role seemed exciting. Three years ago I took a year's leave, and when I came back I asked Sven, our product lead, if I could join his team.

**Viktor:**

It was mostly a coincidence that arose from necessity, I'd say. I've always been very interested in the relationship between a technical solution and its user. I discovered that it was an incredible strength to be able to talk about and explain technical solutions and problems in a simple and understandable way and get people to understand their value. From there, it wasn't far to product development since it worked equally well the other way around—being able to talk about customer needs with more technical teams.

Where do you as a PM make the biggest difference? In other words, if you disappeared, who or what would suffer?

**Nina:**

As a PM, I review data on how our customers use the product, I talk with the commercial teams to understand their challenges and what they hear from customers. The context I get from this I try to convey to the development team so that we prioritize correctly and can more quickly arrive at good solutions. I believe this context exchange would suffer if I disappeared.

**Viktor:**

If I were to remove the product manager role from my current position in a company that's really a product company as an example, I think it would take a little while before it was noticed that it was missing. But fairly quickly, communication gaps would start to emerge that would contribute to our product not meeting expectations from our users, internal and/or external stakeholders. In the long term, there would be a lack of clear direction and cohesion for where the product is heading, which would result in resources being spent on wrong or unnecessary initiatives within the business.

Product Manager, Product Owner, Project Lead. All these similar titles and roles. What's the actual difference and do you really need all of them at every company or for every product?

**Nina:**

I haven't worked at a company with Product Owners and Project Leads, so I don't have any experience with that, but I think it depends a lot on company size, how it's organized, what working method you use, and what type of product you're selling.

**Viktor:**

Dear child has many names, as they say. What you call it depends a lot on the type of company, size, and how you choose to work within the company. Let's start with the difference between Product Manager and Project Manager. A project manager is in charge of a specific project—within the scope of a project, they lead that work compared to a Product Manager who leads the development of a product. As a rule, a product owner has a larger, more long-term perspective in terms of also maintaining, changing, and refining. A product is an organism while a project is a boxed solution; the difference lies in managing those different conditions. Projects are more requirement-driven while products are entirely need-driven.

As a PM you're involved in many parts of the company and product. How do you make sure everyone works toward the same goal and doesn't end up in their own silos?

**Nina:**

It's hard, and it's very easy for each team to only think about their part of the product. I believe a shared goal and a clear product vision are important. I try to remind teams about the goals we have, communicate to them about what's happening in different parts of the company, and encourage teams to talk a lot with other teams and our customers.

**Viktor:**

Involve everyone in the group already during the exploratory phase so there isn't too much "hand-off." I've seen many organizations where they think they're working iteratively but only work iteratively within their own team or work group. Then there's still a hand-off to the next team and so on. The end result becomes something completely different from the original intent. I use the North Star framework to connect the work we do to something measurable and see how we successively improve the core values of the product. So involve as many people as possible from the start, base it on user needs, set measurable goals for what we want to improve, and it's easier to hold the group together and create creative work.

Business, technology, and design are considered the three cornerstones of product development. How do you ensure that design is valued as highly as the other parts and not just as nice packaging?

**Nina:**

I think some of it is about information—getting the organization to understand that design isn't just packaging, but the entire user experience. At Funnel, Design is a very new function, but just as we have a Chief Product Officer and a Chief Technology Officer, we now have a Chief Design Officer, and all three sit in the leadership group. Having Design get an equally strong voice as Product and Development, I believe, makes it easier to ensure it's also valued equally highly.

**Viktor:**

By lifting up and clarifying the value of a good experience for your users and customers throughout the organization. You can have the world's coolest tech, but if no one understands how to use it, you'll miss the mark. Same on the business side. If you haven't understood the end user's needs, you'll have a short-lived product with high maintenance costs. All too many products fail because you try to give them a solution instead of solving their fundamental need. That's where Design comes in. It often starts in your marketing. Explaining what you solve in a good way and setting the right expectations. I work incredibly closely with marketing to create the best possible conditions.

Customers always have lots of often contradictory wishes. How do you choose who to listen to and what to prioritize?

**Nina:**

I usually start by trying to understand what underlying problem they want to solve with their wish, and then find out if other customers have the same problem. I also check what type of customer it is and how well their wish fits our strategy and top priorities. I also talk with the developers to understand what potential solutions exist and how much work it would be to build.

**Viktor:**

I think you should listen to all your customers' wishes, but not equate them with requirements. Instead, figure out what the real need is. Customers have a lot of "wants," but you should work with their "needs" through activities like exploration, user research, prototyping, user testing, and verification. Verifying before you start "building" your product always pays off in the end.

What do you wish you knew at the beginning of your PM career?

**Nina:**

I think I'm still at the beginning of my PM career :)

**Viktor:**

That as a product manager, it's not about you personally coming up with solutions to your customers' needs or thinking up clever features. Your role is to focus on leading and facilitating the process that leads to a better product. I would have liked someone to clearly and simply explain all the important product metrics you have to have a firm grasp on. I often see myself very much as a conductor directing an orchestra where I myself don't play the instruments. I make sure it becomes a very good and enjoyable experience both during rehearsal and for the audience.

Finally, do you have any good tips on books, podcasts, or TED talks for those who want to learn more about the role?

**Nina:**

Marty Cagan wrote the book *Inspired* which describes the PM role well. His blog Insights at www.svpg.com is also very good.

title: I just don’t want to be cool
text:
I just don't want to be cool
I just don't want to be cool
Created on 2020-11-20 09:24
Published on 2020-11-20 09:41

There are many discussions about what UX is and isn't. But there's one thing no one mentions. UX isn't particularly cool. Sure, everyone talks about it. UX trends among job postings and new educational programs are popping up like flowers in a summer meadow. But it lacks all kinds of flair and street cred. It's like being a naprapath—you help people in need but no action movies are made about you, unlike being a police officer. I'm not saying this because I don't like UX; quite the opposite. I see it as a positive trait and a superpower.

I've been on the other side, which purified UX professionals regard with equal parts skepticism and envy. I've had the role of graphic designer, Art Director, and UI designer. I poured my soul into my visual design, slavishly followed all the trends, Dribbble accounts, typography blogs, and super sleek ad campaigns. I did my best to transform dreary wireframes into a living product with its own personality that created a rewarding and inspiring conversation with users. I wanted to touch people and create a link between the screen and the human.

Unfortunately, there was a downside to this cool, creative existence. It was called reality, and it crashed head-on with my grand visions more often than not. It turned out not to be so easy to simultaneously feed my fragile artistic ego, keep imposter syndrome in check, deal with an obliterated graphic manual, and a bunch of meaningless wireframes that were the complete opposite of all innovative design.

At the same time, the client's creative brief usually looked something like this: "It should be sleek, fresh, and modern. Preferably so cool that people want to lick it. But at the same time people should recognize it, and all existing standards and conventions should be followed. And please don't use blue because our competitors have that, or green because our CEO doesn't like green. And no images, and fonts can't cost anything. And let's run with Lorem ipsum for now since our copy isn't done and we don't want to use the old texts."

No wonder many Art Directors become bitter and hate themselves as much as their clients. ;-)

Or you do what I did. Switch to the light side. Leave coolness behind (it's just a construct anyway) and step into the logical, rational world of UX. No more clients who think Gotham and Arial look basically the same so we might as well just use Arial.

Suddenly my designs (yes, I've now started calling wireframes design too) aren't questioned on every point. Relevant questions come up about flows, customer value, and usability. It's like we've found a common language and are striving toward the same goal. My arguments are backed up by facts, tests, interviews, analytics, and can't be shot down as arbitrarily as the ducks in Duck Hunt.

So what? Should everyone become a UX designer and end up with a digital world consisting of gray boxes, arrows, and post-its in Miro? Absolutely not. What's required is

The insight that UX and UI are two sides of the same coin. They're different, sometimes you only see one, sometimes only the other, but they go together and can't be separated. With a common language, it suddenly becomes easier to discuss the visual part, how it connects to UX, contributes to a better experience, and above all, tie it to evidence based on facts, research, and tests.

Leave the coolness to those who still doubt themselves and their design and have coolness as their only life raft. You don't need it anymore; with your new superpowers, you can fly.
